item.title: GovBrief Today #492
item.description: Activists are turning 17,000 pounds of the government's own printed Epstein files into a public archive in Washington, with Trump's name on the door. The "Trump and Epstein Memorial Reading Room" opens in days, timed to his birthday next week, and holds 3,400 volumes. Every page the DOJ released is now printed and bound, there to read in your hands instead of on a screen that scrolls it away. The fury over the rape and abuse of children was never a left or right question, and it remains one of the few things this country still agrees on. After stonewalling for years, Trump had no choice but to sign the law that got these files released. Now they sit in the capital under his name and Epstein's. I've emailed 7,716 stories over 492 straight nights, free and without ads. The algorithm can't show them all to you. Add your email address at govbrief.today and I will. #GovBriefToday #Resist Subscribe (It's free!): https://gbounacos.substack.com/ Today's full brief: https://go.govbrief.today/260605-full [1] https://go.govbrief.today/anachronistic-dream [2] https://go.govbrief.today/iran-war-june6 [3] https://go.govbrief.today/hegseth-dday [4] https://go.govbrief.today/krishnan-leaving [5] https://go.govbrief.today/buyer-pardon [6] https://go.govbrief.today/epstein-reading-room Epstein Timeline: https://go.govbrief.today/epstein-crimes Subscribe (It's free!): https://gbounacos.substack.com/
item.dc:creator: GeorgeBounacos

item.dc:creator: Che07

item.title: My old train was derailed and kicked me in the feels
item.description:
A truck driver hauling a huge John Deere farm tractor decided to run the crossing instead of being inconvenienced for three fucking minutes. Congratulations, your selfish decision killed your son and caused millions of dollars worth of damages that traumatized at least two other men for life. The crew is okay ish physically. I messaged someone I used to work with (one of the few that actually loved their job and was a blast to work with) to make sure he wasn't on this train. He and I ran this route many times and very likely could've been one of us had I kept working there and the dice rolled slightly different. This train was the one I was assigned to the most so it really hits home for me. My biggest fear was being tipped sideways or toppling off a bridge at night. The conductor side is facing up which means that poor dude got thrashed around on there amidst all the mangling steel and terrifying noises. This was the worst incident I believe in many years.
I was driving back home after turning in my gear. I can't describe the swelling melancholy feeling in my chest when I saw my train for the last time. The crew even gave me a wailing horn and a wave as a send off. I was starting to almost feel like one of the guys but I missed my real family and had to walk away. I parted on good terms and fulfilled a childhood dream of being a real mainline conductor. This video was taken within a couple miles of the derailment. Track speed is 40mph because we didn't have signals aka "dark territory". Not as fast as the class 1's, but fast enough
The 502 was my personal favorite and the 513 a favorite to many others (wasn't a fan of its radio in the conductor seat). It's known as a 'heritage unit' painted in the livery of one of its predecessor railroads. For such a small railroad, the CEO is one of the most passionate railroaders I've ever heard of and the story of this operation growing into what it is today is truly inspiring. In the earlier days, they would start out with 6 locos and be lucky to have two running by the time they got to their destination.
May ish 2020. Same route. The 513 is even the second engine. Not sure if she'll run again. The 514 left us stranded when I was a student conductor ? According to the engineers, the teen series in the 500s kinda sucked. The 500-510 were beasts.
Notice how you can see the crossing from very far away.
Similar highway, not exact but an illustration on how simple the terrain is. No excuses for that derailment. None.
I'm fortunate enough to have railroaded for seven years and several different territories without being too traumatized. I can count less than five times I was almost severely injured or worse. Never hit anyone but came really close. This job was the most challenging, proudest highlights in my adult life. The lifestyle isn't for everyone. We'd rather see someone leave with pride and chase some stability before it's too late. This is a very specific set of skills that don't really transfer well to other jobs. I was fortunate enough to have worked in freight car repair before being a conductor so the welding, torch, and super forklift training were more than worth it. I hope this adds some perspective and that you all continue to drive safely. And use ya damn turn signals properly!
item.dc:creator: cellocalypse

item.title: Sitting in the same seat as my grandmother did 8 years ago and having the same beer in her memory!
item.description: She passed away a few years ago and we were able to make it back to recreate my favorite photo of her. She was always down to have a good time! Miss her every day <3
